mәtәklәwγәt has prefix 'mәtә-' (we), root 'klәw' (tie up), and suffix '-γәt' (you sg.)
inekәlwәrkәnitәk contains progressive marker '-rkә-' and patterns suggest 'you (pl.)' tying 'me' up

answer: He is seeing you (pl.); They are seeing you (pl.); He is seeing me; They are seeing me
reasoning: nel?urkәnitәk has prefix 'ne-' (he/they), root 'l?u' (see), progressive '-rkә-', allowing multiple interpretations
I tie him up: tә- (I) + kәlw (tie up) + -γʔen (him)
You (sg.) are seeing us: lә- (you sg.) + l?u (see) + -rkә- (progressive) + -mәk (us)
You (pl.) sniff him: no prefix for you (pl.) + jŋo (sniff) + -γʔen (him)
He is tying them up: ne- (he) + kәlw (tie up) + -rkә- (progressive) + -net (them)

answer: Chukchi verbs have the structure: Subject prefix + Verb root + (Progressive marker) + Object suffix. Subject prefixes: tә- 'I', lә- 'you sg.', ne- 'he/they', mәtә- 'we', ∅ 'you pl.'. Verb roots: kәlw/klәw 'tie up', l?u 'see', jŋo 'sniff'. Progressive: -rkә-. Object suffixes: -mәk 'us', -tәk 'you pl./me', -γәt 'you sg.', -net 'them', -γʔi 'me', -n/-γʔen 'him'.
reasoning: Analysis of all examples shows consistent polysynthetic structure with prefixes for subjects and suffixes for objects
